lead:
**Libre Cloud** is my zero-trust self-hosted infrastructure project running on Oracle Cloud's Always Free tier. The architecture is designed to route all incoming public traffic through Cloudflare's network for DDoS protection, IP masking, and SSL termination. From there, traffic is directed into an Oracle Cloud Virtual Machine running a Caddy/Nginx reverse proxy that handles request routing for both public endpoints and secure, WireGuard-private services.

I named the project 'Libre Cloud' to reflect its open-source philosophy: every piece of the software stack is free, the hosting infrastructure is always-free cloud compute, and the personal domain is configured to respect user privacy. It serves as the primary deployment target for my [[privacy-stack|Privacy Stack]], hosting utility systems such as Vaultwarden for password management, Joplin Server for synchronized notes, and SearXNG for private search queries.

The physical deployment is constrained by cloud resource limits. While the initial blueprint targeted a `VM.Standard.A1.Flex` instance with 4 ARM OCPUs and 24 GB of RAM, persistent 'out of capacity' errors in the OCI Hyderabad region forced an indefinite suspension of that plan. Consequently, the entire infrastructure runs on a single `VM.Standard.E2.1.Micro` AMD instance with 1 OCPU and exactly 1 GB of RAM.

Operating within a 1 GB RAM envelope requires strict resource management. Heavy management interfaces like Coolify (which consumes 500 MB to 1 GB RAM on its own) are unusable, and resource-intensive applications like Immich, Mailcow, and Jellyfin are impossible to deploy. Instead, I focus on ultra-lightweight Docker services, prioritizing WireGuard (~5 MB RAM), Pi-hole (~50 MB RAM), and Vaultwarden (~50 MB RAM).

Despite these compute limitations, the zero-trust design remains robust. Private administrative services are entirely hidden from the public internet, requiring authentication through a WireGuard VPN tunnel before any requests can reach the backend reverse proxy.

sections.title: Current Reality — What I Actually Have

content.title: Status: Blocked / Severely Constrained
content.content: My target VM was VM.Standard.A1.Flex — Oracle's 4 OCPU, 24 GB RAM ARM Ampere instance, free forever. I hit persistent 'out of capacity' errors in the Hyderabad region after repeated attempts over months. The ARM plan is abandoned for now. What I actually have: 1x VM.Standard.E2.1.Micro — 1 OCPU, 1 GB RAM.

I designed the architecture so nothing is directly exposed to the internet without passing through Cloudflare and the reverse proxy layer. Services are split into three visibility tiers: public (accessible to anyone via Cloudflare), private (accessible only through a WireGuard VPN tunnel), and ARM-only (requiring the unprovisioned A1.Flex VM).

content.title: My architecture flow
content.content: Internet → Cloudflare (DNS + DDoS + IP masking) → Oracle Cloud VM (1 GB Micro) → Reverse proxy (Caddy/nginx) → [Public: Portfolio, Pi-hole DoH] / [Private (WireGuard-gated): Vaultwarden, Joplin Server, Gitea]

rows.Purpose: Requires 24 GB RAM — indefinitely on hold

content.content:
Oracle Cloud's Always Free tier is uniquely generous compared to AWS, GCP, and Azure free tiers. The headline offering is the **ARM Ampere A1.Flex** instance: 4 OCPU and 24 GB RAM — free forever. For comparison, AWS Free Tier gives a t2.micro (1 vCPU, 1 GB) for only 12 months. Oracle's AMD Micro is comparable but always-free in perpetuity, and the ARM Ampere would be dramatically more capable.

The catch is capacity availability: the Hyderabad region ARM Ampere has been perennially 'out of capacity'. This is a widely-reported issue in the Oracle Cloud community — not unique to me. Other regions would require a separate account setup. I've been stuck on this constraint for months.
The plan is sound. The hardware just never materialised.
content.author: Project notes, April 2026
